elements.sent-2411-001: In the absence of gravity, Coleman's theorem states that the $O(4)$-symmetric instanton solution, which is regular at the origin and exponentially decays at infinity, gives the lowest action.
elements.sent-2411-002: Perturbatively, this implies that any small deformation from $O(4)$-symmetry gives a larger action.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this letter we investigate the possibility of extending this theorem to the situation where the $O(4)$-symmetric instanton is singular, provided that the action is finite.
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, we show a general form of the potential around the origin, which realizes a singular instanton with finite action.
elements.sent-2411-005: We then discuss a concrete example in which this situation is realized, and analyze non-trivial anisotropic deformations around the solution perturbatively.
elements.sent-2411-006: Intriguingly, in contrast to the case of Coleman's instantons, we find that there exists a deformed solution that has the same action as the one for the $O(4)$-symmetric solution up to the second order in perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our result implies that there exist non-$O(4)$-symmetric solutions with finite action beyond Coleman's instantons, and gives rise to the possibility of the existence of a non-$O(4)$-symmetric instanton with a lower action.
elements.sent-2411-008: The study of instanton solutions in quantum field theory has long been a cornerstone in understanding non-perturbative phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-009: Instantons, as classical solutions to the equations of motion in Euclidean space, offer physical insights into the tunneling process from a metastable (false) vacuum to a stable (true) vacuum~\cite{Coleman:1977py,Callan:1977pt .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In a model with a single field $\Phi$, one employs the semiclassical approximation to express the decay rate $\Gamma$ per unit time per unit volume ($\mathcal{V}$) as \begin{equation}\label{eq:decay_rate} \Gamma/V = A\,e^{-B[\Phi]} \;,\end{equation} where $A$ is the prefactor that contains $\hbar$ corrections, and $B[\Phi]$ is the bounce action defined in terms of the Euclidean action $S[\Phi]$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:bounce_action} B[\Phi] \equiv S[\Phi] - S[\Phi_{\rm FV}] \;,\end{equation} with $\Phi_{\rm FV}$ being the location of the false vacuum.
elements.sent-2411-011: Note that in the semiclassical limit one evaluates the action $S[\Phi]$ on a classical solution subjected to appropriate boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-012: Indeed, it was mathematically proven in \cite{Coleman:1977th that in the absence of gravity the $O(4)$-symmetric solutions result in the minimal bounce action (the most probable tunneling process) under certain conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Those solutions were actually assumed to satisfy the boundary conditions, \begin{equation}\label{eq:con_coleman} \Phi(\rho)\big|_{\rho \rightarrow \infty} = \Phi_{\rm FV} \;, \quad \frac{d\Phi(\rho)}{d\rho} \bigg|_{\rho \rightarrow 0}= 0 \;,\end{equation} where $\rho$ denotes the radial coordinate in the 4-dimensional Euclidean space.
elements.sent-2411-014: The second condition in (\ref{eq:con_coleman ) guarantees the regularity of the solution at the center of the true-vacuum bubble.}
elements.sent-2411-015: We call Euclidean solutions that satisfy the condition (\ref{eq:con_coleman ) the Coleman instantons.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Recently, in \cite{Mukhanov:2021rpp,Mukhanov:2021kat a class of (unbounded) potentials in an arbitrary spacetime dimension was identified, for which the Coleman instantons do not exist.}
elements.sent-2411-017: See \cite{Mukhanov:2020pau,Mukhanov:2020wim,Mukhanov:2021ggy for new instantons which do not satisfy the Coleman boundary conditions, Eq.~(\ref{eq:con_coleman ).\footnote{The authors introduced the UV cutoff to make the action finite.}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this letter we investigate another case where $O(4)$-symmetric Euclidean solutions are singular but their action remains finite.\footnote{This situation is reminiscent of the Hawking-Turok instanton \cite{Hawking:1998bn where gravity is taken into account.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: In this case, the instanton solution diverges as $\rho \rightarrow 0$, but the action is finite.
elements.sent-2411-020: Namely, we consider $O(4)$-symmetric instantons that do not obey the second condition of Eq.~(\ref{eq:con_coleman ), hence they are singular at the true-vacuum bubble.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Also, similar to \cite{Mukhanov:2021rpp,Mukhanov:2021kat , we are interested in an unbounded potential, which violates one of the assumptions for Coleman's theorem.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Moreover, we explicitly show that even if small and regular deformations are taken into account, the total action is still given by that computed on the $O(4)$-symmetric ones.
elements.sent-2411-023: We do not include gravity in this letter.\footnote{We do not consider the backreaction on the metric due to scalar fluctuations.. Setup.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Let us start with a real scalar field model with the Euclidean action, \begin{equation}\label{eq:action_Eu} S = \int d^4x \sqrt{g} \bigg[\frac{1}{2}\,g^{\mu\nu} \partial_\mu \Phi(x) \partial_\nu \Phi(x) + V(\Phi) \bigg] \;,\end{equation} where $V(\Phi)$ is the potential.
elements.sent-2411-025: We consider the 4d flat Euclidean metric, \begin{equation}\label{eq:flat_metric} ds^2 = d\tau^2 + d\vec{x}^2 = d\rho^2 + \rho^2(d\theta^2 + \sin^2\theta\,d\Omega_2^2) \;,\end{equation} where $\tau$ is a Euclidean time, $\rho \equiv \sqrt{\tau^2 + \vec{x ^2}$ and $\mathrm{d}\Omega_2^2 \equiv \mathrm{d}\chi^2 + \sin^2 \chi\,\mathrm{d}\phi^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Notice that although the metric after the second equality in \eqref{eq:flat_metric is expressed in the manifestly $O(4)$-symmetric form, the model may admit solutions which are not $O(4)$-symmetric.}
elements.sent-2411-027: It may also admit solutions which are singular at $\rho=0$.
elements.sent-2411-028: From the action (\ref{eq:action_Eu ), the equation of motion (EoM) of $\Phi$ reads \begin{equation}\Box \Phi - \frac{dV(\Phi)}{d\Phi} = 0 \;, \label{eq:EOM_phi}\end{equation} where $\Box \equiv g^{\mu\nu}\partial_\mu \partial_\nu$.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The EoM above in general describes dynamics of instanton solution with prescribed boundary conditions: one at the origin of the true-vacuum bubble ($\rho = 0$) and the other at false vacuum ($\rho \rightarrow \infty$).
elements.sent-2411-030: In order to construct the potential $V(\Phi)$, we first consider the $O(4)$-symmetric instanton: $\Phi = \bar{\Phi (\rho)$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Eq.~(\ref{eq:EOM_phi ) then reduces to \begin{equation}\bar{\Phi}'' + \frac{3}{\rho} \bar{\Phi}' - \frac{dV}{d\Phi}\bigg|_{\Phi = \bar{\Phi}} = 0\;, \label{eq:EOM_phi_zeroth}\end{equation} where $' \equiv {\rm d /{\rm d}\rho$.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The corresponding action reads \begin{equation}\label{eq:action_zero} S_0 = 2\pi^2 \int_0^\infty d\rho~\rho^3~\bigg[\frac{1}{2}\bar{\Phi}'^2 + V(\bar{\Phi}) \bigg] \;,\end{equation} where the factor $2\pi^2$ was obtained from the integrals over the angular variables.
elements.sent-2411-033: Here we allow an instanton to be singular at the origin, or equivalently the true vacuum is located infinitely far away in field space, provided that the action remains finite.\footnote{Note that there could be a cuspy solution at the origin with the first derivative of the scalar field remains finite.}
elements.sent-2411-034: From (\ref{eq:action_zero ), we see that if $\bar{\Phi '^2(\rho)$ blows up faster than $1/\rho^3$ when $\rho \rightarrow 0$, the action will inevitably diverge.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Therefore, the finiteness of the action yields the conditions on the behaviors of $\bar{\Phi '$ and $\bar{\Phi}$ at the origin.}
elements.sent-2411-036: From the above reasoning, we find that the general form of the potential as $\rho \rightarrow 0$ with finite action is \begin{equation}\label{eq:potential_gen_con} V(\Phi) = -\Lambda^4\exp\bigg(\frac{2\Phi}{\Phi_\star}\bigg) \;,\end{equation} where $\Lambda$ and $\Phi_\star$ are arbitrary constants.
elements.sent-2411-037: One can straightforwardly verify that Eq.~(\ref{eq:potential_gen_con ) leads to logarithmic divergence of $\bar{\Phi}$ at the origin, but it gives finite action.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Indeed, the form (\ref{eq:potential_gen_con ) violates one of the assumptions of the theorem \cite{Coleman:1977th , so that it does not apply in our case.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Notice that the potential (\ref{eq:potential_gen_con ) has not been considered in the literature, and here we do not need to introduce a UV cutoff since our integral over $\rho$ is finite.}
elements.sent-2411-040: See \cite{Sasaki:2024 for discussion of other types of singularities of $\bar{\Phi}$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Note also that it is straightforward to show that the potential (\ref{eq:potential_gen_con ) is generic in $d > 2$ dimensions.. Small anisotropic deformation.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Let us analyze a small anisotropic deformation $\delta \Phi$ of the $O(4)$-symmetric instanton, \begin{equation}\label{eq:ansatz} \Phi(x^\mu) = \bar{\Phi}(\rho) + \epsilon\, \delta\Phi(\rho, \vec{\theta}\,) \;,\end{equation} where $\epsilon$ is the anisotropic parameter satisfying $\epsilon\ll1$.
elements.sent-2411-043: Notice that $\delta\Phi$ can generally depend on both $\rho$ and the angular variables, $\vec{\theta = \{\theta, \chi, \phi\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The solution $\delta\Phi(\rho, \vec{\theta}\,)$ can be decomposed as \begin{equation}\delta\Phi(\rho, \vec{\theta}\,) = \sum_{L,M} A_{LM}(\rho)\,Y_L^M(\vec{\theta}\,) \;, \label{eq:sol_PHi1}\end{equation} where the function $Y_L^M(\vec{\theta}\,)$ is the 3-dimensional spherical harmonics \cite{doi:10.1063/1.527513 , obeying \begin{equation}\Delta_{S^{3}} Y_L^M(\vec{\theta}\,) = -L(L + 2) Y^M_L(\vec{\theta}\,) \;,\end{equation} with $\Delta_{S^{3 }$ the Laplacian of a 3-sphere and $M$ a multi-index characterizing the magnetic quantum numbers $\{m_\phi, m_\chi, m_\theta\}$ satisfying $|m_\phi| \leq m_\chi \leq L \equiv m_\theta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The radial component $A_L(\rho)$ then satisfies \begin{equation}\label{eq:A(rho)} A''_L + \frac{3}{\rho} A'_L - \bigg[ \frac{\de ^2 V}{\de\Phi^2}\bigg|_{\bar{\Phi}} + \frac{L(L + 2)}{\rho^2} \bigg]A_L = 0 \;,\end{equation} where we have omitted the index $M$.
elements.sent-2411-046: This equation determines the dynamics of fluctuations $A_L$ on the background $\bar{\Phi}$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Notice that the modes with $L = 0$ and $L = 1$ are not physical, see \cite{Sasaki:2024 for detailed discussion.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Straightforwardly, inserting (\ref{eq:ansatz ) into (\ref{eq:action_Eu ), one arrives at the action up to $\mathcal{O (\epsilon^2)$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:action_Phi1} S = S_0[\bar{\Phi}] + \epsilon^2 S_2[\delta\Phi] \;,\end{equation} where $S_0$ is given by Eq.~(\ref{eq:action_zero ) and \begin{equation}\label{eq:action_Phi2} S_2 =\frac{1}{2} \int d^4x \sqrt{g}\,\bigg[(\partial \delta\Phi)^2 + \frac{\de^2V}{\de\Phi^2}\bigg|_{\bar{\Phi}} \delta \Phi^2 \bigg] \;.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Note that we have performed the integration by parts and used Eq.~(\ref{eq:EOM_phi_zeroth ) to obtain (\ref{eq:action_Phi1 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Hence, using the solution (\ref{eq:sol_PHi1 ) in the action $S_2$ gives \begin{equation}S_2 = \sum_{L \geq 2} \int d\rho\, L_{2L} \label{eq:second_actio_AL} \;,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:integrand_A_L} L_{2L} \equiv \frac{\rho^3}{2} \bigg\{A_L'^2 + \bigg[\frac{L(L+2)}{\rho^2} + \frac{\de^2 V}{\de \Phi^2}\bigg|_{\bar{\Phi}}\bigg] A_L^2\bigg\} \;,\end{equation} and we have used the orthogonality condition for spherical harmonics.}
elements.sent-2411-051: For regular $A_L$, the action (\ref{eq:second_actio_AL ) can be integrated as \begin{equation}S_2 = \pi^2 \sum_{L \geq 2} \rho^3 A_L(\rho) A'_L(\rho) \bigg|_{\rho = 0}^{\rho = \infty} \;, \label{eq:s_2_phi_1}\end{equation} where we have performed an integration by parts and used Eq.~(\ref{eq:A(rho) ).}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Notice that the above expression does not hold for the singular $A_L$.\footnote{We discuss this point in our separate work \cite{Sasaki:2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Note that in the singular case, one may introduce the cutoff to regularize the action as $\rho \rightarrow 0$, see e.g.~\cite{Mukhanov:2021ggy .}
elements.sent-2411-054: We see that the action $S_2$ is always finite as long as $A_L$ is regular everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-055: Moreover, it indicates that $S_2$ is solely determined by the behaviors of $A_L$ at the origin and infinity.
elements.sent-2411-056: As mentioned above, our model is beyond Coleman theorem \cite{Coleman:1977th since the $O(4)$-symmetric solution $\bar{\Phi}$ is assumed to be singular at $\rho = 0$ and the potential is not admissible everywhere.}
elements.sent-2411-057: It may be interesting to have a rigorous proof of the least action with a singular instanton, we leave this to future work.
elements.sent-2411-058: In the following, we present a concrete example which explicitly realizes the case we are interested in.
elements.sent-2411-060: We study an example, where the potential is \begin{equation}\label{eq:piecewise_potential} \frac{V(\Phi)}{\alpha^4} = \left\{  -\Phi_{\star}^4 \exp(2 \Phi/\Phi_\star)\;, \Phi \geq 0 V_{1}(\Phi) \;, \Phi_2 \leq \Phi \leq 0 V_{2}(\Phi) \;, \Phi \leq \Phi_2  \right. \;,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}V_{1}(\Phi) \equiv -\frac{1}{2}m_1^2(\Phi - \Phi_{\rm P})^2 - \Lambda_1^4 \;, V_{2}(\Phi) \equiv \frac{1}{2}m_2^2(\Phi - \Phi_{\rm M})^2 - \Lambda_2^4 \;,\end{equation} where $\Lambda$, $m_1$, $m_2$, $\Phi_{\rm P}$, $\Phi_{\rm M}$, $\Lambda_1$, $\Lambda_2$, $\Phi_\star$ are parameters with mass dimension, $\alpha$ is an overall dimensionless parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Notice that $\Phi_{\rm P}$ and $\Phi_{\rm M}$ denote the locations of the maximum and the minimum (false vacuum) in $V_1$ and $V_2$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-062: Then, imposing the continuity conditions for the potential and its first derivative at $\Phi = 0$ and $\Phi = \Phi_2$, one can fix the parameters $\{\Lambda_1, \Lambda_2, \Phi_{\rm P , \Phi_2\}$ in terms of $\{m_1, m_2 ,\Phi_{\rm M}\}$ such that \begin{equation}\label{paracon} \begin{aligned} \Phi_{\rm P} = -\frac{2\Phi_\star^3}{m_1^2} \;, \Phi_2 = -\frac{2 \Phi_\star^3}{m_1^2 + m_2^2}\bigg(1 - \frac{m_2^2\Phi_{\rm M}}{2\Phi_\star^3}\bigg) \;, \Lambda_1 = \Phi_\star \bigg(1 - \frac{\Phi_\star^2}{m_1^2}\bigg) \;, \Lambda_2 = -\frac{2\Phi_\star^3 }{(m_1^2+m_2^2)} \bigg[1 - \bigg(1+\frac{2\Phi_{\rm M}}{\Phi_\star}\bigg)\frac{m_2^2}{2 \Phi_\star^2} - \bigg(1 + \frac{m_2^2 \Phi_{\rm M}^2}{2 \Phi_\star^4}\bigg)\frac{m_1^2}{2\Phi_\star^2}\bigg]\;. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Moreover, the location of the false vacuum must fall into the regime of $V_2$, which implies that $\Phi_{\rm M < \Phi_2 < 0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: This gives the condition\footnote{One can straightforwardly verify that the condition (\ref{eq:con_m1 ) automatically leads to $\Phi_2 \leq \Phi_{\rm P < 0$.. , \begin{equation}\label{eq:con_m1} 2 + \frac{m_1^2 \Phi_{\rm M}}{ \Phi_\star^3} < 0 \;.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The above condition must be satisfied for any value of $m_1$ and $\Phi_{\rm M}$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Notice that in our setup we have $\Phi_{\rm M < 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-066: For concreteness, in Fig.~\ref{fig:piecewise_potential we plot the potential (\ref{eq:piecewise_potential ) with $m_1 = 2.05\Phi_\star$, $m_2 = 1.45\Phi_\star$, $\Phi_{\rm M = -0.54\Phi_\star$ and $\alpha = 0.5$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Note that these values of parameters are chosen such that all the matching conditions of the background solutions are satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-068: In the plot, the red solid line corresponds to the exponential potential, whereas the blue dashed and the green dot-dashed lines refer to the potentials $V_1$ and $V_2$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-069: The two black dots represent the matching points at $\Phi = 0$ and $\Phi = \Phi_2 = -0.50 \Phi_\star$.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{Figures/Potential_PieceWise_Quadratic_new} \caption{~$V(\Phi)/(\alpha\Phi_\star)^4$ in (\ref{eq:piecewise_potential}) as a function of $\Phi$ with $m_1 = 2.05\Phi_\star$, $m_2 = 1.45 \Phi_\star$ and $\Phi_{\rm M} = -0.54\Phi_\star$. The solid red line represents the exponential potential, while the blue dashed and the green dot-dashed lines refer to the potentials $V_1$ and $V_2$ in Eq.~(\ref{eq:piecewise_potential}) respectively. The two black points in the plot refer to the two matching locations at $\Phi = 0$ and at $\Phi = \Phi_2 = -0.50\Phi_\star$. The false vacuum for this particular choice of parameters is located at $\Phi_{\rm M} = -0.54\Phi_\star$.} \label{fig:piecewise_potential}
elements.sent-2411-070: At the background level, in each regime of the potential (\ref{eq:piecewise_potential ) the solutions $\bar{\Phi}$ can be obtained analytically with their integration constants.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Throughout this example, we do not use the thin-wall approximation.
elements.sent-2411-072: In the exponential-potential regime, solving Eq.~(\ref{eq:EOM_phi_zeroth ) we find that \begin{equation}\label{eq:bg_exp} \bar{\Phi}(\tilde{\rho}) = -\Phi_\star\log(\tilde{\rho}) \,;~0<\tilde{\rho}\leqslant1 \,,\end{equation} where $\tilde{\rho \equiv \alpha^2 \Phi_\star \rho$.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Notice that in (\ref{eq:bg_exp ) we focus on the singular behavior, so that the regular behavior is disregarded.\footnote{Of course, once the condition $\Phi'_0 \rightarrow \infty$ at $\rho = 0$ is imposed, $\bar{\Phi}$ is uniquely determined by Eq.~(\ref{eq:bg_exp ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: In the $V_1$ regime the solution reads \begin{equation}\bar{\Phi} (\tilde{\rho}) = - \frac{2\Phi_\star^3}{m_1^2} + \frac{c_1}{\tilde{\rho}} J_1\big(\frac{m_1}{\Phi_\star}\tilde{\rho}\big) + \frac{c_2}{\tilde{\rho}}Y_1\big(\frac{m_1}{\Phi_\star} \tilde{\rho}\big) \,; \nonumber 1\leqslant\tilde{\rho}\leqslant\tilde{\rho}_2 \,,\end{equation} where $J_1$ and $Y_1$ are Bessel functions of the first and second kinds respectively, and $c_1$, $c_2$ are constants.
elements.sent-2411-075: In the $V_2$ regime we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:sol_phi_0_V2} \bar{\Phi}(\tilde{\rho}) = \Phi_{\rm M} + \frac{c_3}{\tilde{\rho}} K_1\big(\frac{m_2}{\Phi_\star}\tilde{\rho}\big) + \frac{c_4}{\tilde{\rho}}I_1\big( \frac{m_2}{\Phi_\star}\tilde{\rho}\big) \,;~\tilde{\rho}\geqslant\tilde{\rho}_2 \,,\end{equation} where $K_1$ and $I_1$ are modified Bessel functions of the first and second kinds respectively\footnote{The Bessel functions $K_1(z)$, $I_1(z)$ behaves as $e^{-z}$ and $e^{z}$ as $z \rightarrow \infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: We see that in order for $\bar{\Phi}$ asymptotically goes to $\Phi_{\rm M}$ as $\tilde{\rho \rightarrow \infty$, we impose $c_4 = 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Then, using the continuity conditions at $\tilde{\rho = 1$ ($\bar{\Phi = 0$) the constants $c_1$ and $c_2$ are fixed in terms of $m_1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: At the second matching point $\tilde{\rho = \tilde{\rho _2$ ($\bar{\Phi = \Phi_2$), one uses the continuities of both $\bar{\Phi}$ and $\bar{\Phi '$ to obtain \begin{equation}\bigg(2 + \frac{m_1^2 \Phi_{\rm M}}{\Phi_\star^3}\bigg) H_1 + H_2 = 0 \;,\end{equation} where $H_1$ and $H_2$ are functions of $m_1$, $m_2$, and $\tilde{\rho _2$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Hence, $\Phi_{\rm M}$ is also fixed by these parameters.
elements.sent-2411-080: Therefore, inserting the expression for $\Phi_{\rm M}$ into (\ref{eq:con_m1 ), we obtain the inequality, \begin{equation}\label{eq:con_bg} H_3\left(\frac{m_1}{\Phi_\star}, \frac{m_2}{\Phi_\star}, \tilde{\rho}_2 \right) \leq 0 \;.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Here we omit the full expressions of $H_1$, $H_2$ and $H_3$ due to their length.
elements.sent-2411-082: In addition to (\ref{eq:con_bg ), we identify the solution \eqref{eq:sol_phi_0_V2 at $\tilde{\rho = \tilde{\rho _2$ with $\Phi_2$ in (\ref{paracon ), i.e., \begin{equation}\bar{\Phi}(\tilde{\rho}_2) = \Phi_2 \,.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: We see that for any values of $\tilde{\rho _2 > 1$ the above conditions must be satisfied in order for the background solution in all regimes can be smoothly connected and asymptotically goes to the false vacuum $\Phi_{\rm M}$.}

elements.sent-2411-086: After fixing $\tilde{\rho _2$ and imposing the above condition our free parameters are $m_1$ and $\alpha$.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{Figures/Contour_Piecewise_quadratic_rho2=5_new} \caption{~Parameter space of $m_1/\Phi_\star$ and $m_2/\Phi_\star$. The condition (\ref{eq:con_bg}) is satisfied in the yellow region. The green line refers to the condition $\bar{\Phi}(\tilde{\rho}_2) = \Phi_2$, the red curve corresponds to the condition $c_8 = 0$. We choose $\tilde{\rho}_2 = 5$ and $\alpha = 0.5$. The black dot is $\{2.05,1.45\}$, for which the fluctuation is regular everywhere (their corresponding $\Phi_2$ and $\Phi_{\rm M}$ are respectively given by $\Phi_2 = -0.50\Phi_\star, \Phi_{\rm M} = -0.54\Phi_\star$).} \label{fig:contour_plot_piecewise}
elements.sent-2411-087: Having determined the background O(4)-symmetric solution $\bar{\Phi}$, now we turn to analyze the small deformation $\delta\Phi(\rho, \vec{\theta}\,)$ in \eqref{eq:ansatz .}
elements.sent-2411-088: In order to solve its radical components $A_L$, we introduce a dimensionless variable $f_L \equiv \tilde{\rho ^{3/2 A_L/\Phi_\star$.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: In the exponential regime of potential \eqref{eq:piecewise_potential , solving (\ref{eq:A(rho) ) gives \begin{equation}\label{eq:sol_f_expo} f_L(\tilde{\rho}) = a_1 \tilde{\rho}^{\frac{1}{2} + \sqrt{j}} + a_2 \tilde{\rho}^{\frac{1}{2} -\sqrt{j}}\,;~0<\tilde{\rho}\leqslant1\,,\end{equation} where $a_1$, $a_2$ are constants and $j \equiv (L - 1)(L + 3)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Here we focus on the regular behavior at the origin, i.e., $a_2 = 0$, in order for the action to be finite.

elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{Figures/Background_and_Fluctuation_Piecewise_quadratic_new} \caption{~The solutions for $\bar{\Phi}$ (solid line) and $f_2(\tilde{\rho})$ (dashed line). The red, blue and green colors refer to the solutions in the exponential potential, $V_1$ and $V_2$ regimes, respectively. We set $\alpha = 0.5$, $m_1 = 2.05\Phi_\star$ and $m_2 = 1.45\Phi_\star$, so that $\Phi_2 = -0.50\Phi_\star$ and $\Phi_{\rm M} = -0.54\Phi_\star$. The two matching points (black dots) are located at $\tilde{\rho} = 1$ and $\tilde{\rho}_2 = 5$.} \label{fig:fluctuation_piecewise}

elements.sent-2411-113: Therefore, the total action (\ref{eq:action_Phi1 ) is the same as the one evaluated for the $O(4)$-symmetric instanton.}

elements.sent-2411-115: Our result implies that there exist non-$O(4)$-symmetric solutions with finite action beyond Coleman's instantons.. Discussion.
elements.sent-2411-116: We studied a possibility of extending the theorem \cite{Coleman:1977th to the case where the $O(4)$-symmetric instanton solution exhibits a singular behavior at $\rho = 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-117: In particular, we considered the case in which the solution does not satisfy Coleman's condition (\ref{eq:con_coleman ).}
elements.sent-2411-118: We further investigated a small anisotropic deformation around the singular $O(4)$-symmetric instanton.
elements.sent-2411-119: We proposed a concrete example with a piecewise potential given by (\ref{eq:piecewise_potential ).}
elements.sent-2411-120: We then analyzed the solutions for $\bar{\Phi}$, that is singular at $\rho = 0$ and goes to false vacuum as $\rho \rightarrow \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-121: Finally, we studied the dynamics of small anisotropic deformation and found that there exists a small and regular deformation around the singular $O(4)$-symmetric solution, which does not contribute to the total bounce action.
elements.sent-2411-122: This opens several possibilities to study other cases, where the Coleman's theorem may not apply.
elements.sent-2411-123: Moreover, this can be extended to the case where gravity is present (a general theorem is not known yet), see e.g. \cite{Cohn:1998et,Gratton:1999ya,Dunne:2006bt,Ai:2023yce .}
elements.sent-2411-124: In our separate work \cite{Sasaki:2024 we will discuss other types of singular behaviors in addition to logarithmic divergence, and the generic form of the instanton potential.. Acknowledgements.}

elements.sent-2411-127: We call Euclidean solutions that satisfy the condition (\ref{eq:con_coleman ) Coleman's instantons.}

elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{Figures/Potential_PieceWise_Quadratic} \caption{~$V(\Phi)/(\alpha\Phi_\star)^4$ in (\ref{eq:piecewise_potential}) as a function of $\Phi$ with $m_1 = 0.98\Phi_\star$, $m_2 = 7.11 \Phi_\star$ and $\Phi_{\rm M} = -2.35\Phi_\star$. The solid red line represents the exponential potential, while the blue dashed and the green dot-dashed lines refer to the potentials $V_1$ and $V_2$ in Eq.~(\ref{eq:piecewise_potential}) respectively. The two black points in the plot refer to the two matching locations at $\Phi = 0$ and at $\Phi = \Phi_2 = -2.34\Phi_\star$. The false vacuum for this particular choice of parameters is located at $\Phi_{\rm M} = -2.35\Phi_\star$.} \label{fig:piecewise_potential}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{Figures/Contour_Piecewise_quadratic_rho2=5_two_plots.pdf} \caption{~Parameter space of $m_1/\Phi_\star$ and $m_2/\Phi_\star$. The condition (\ref{eq:con_bg}) is satisfied in the yellow region. The green line refers to the condition $\bar{\Phi}(\tilde{\rho}_2) = \Phi_2$, the red curve corresponds to the condition $c_8 = 0$. We choose $\tilde{\rho}_2 = 5$ and $\alpha = 0.5$. The black dot is $\{0.98,7.11\}$, for which the fluctuation is regular everywhere (their corresponding $\Phi_2$ and $\Phi_{\rm M}$ are respectively given by $\Phi_2 = -2.34\Phi_\star, \Phi_{\rm M} = -2.35\Phi_\star$). The small figure explicitly shows the intersection point.} \label{fig:contour_plot_piecewise}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{Figures/Background_and_Fluctuation_Piecewise_quadratic} \caption{~The solutions for $\bar{\Phi}$ (solid line) and $f_2(\tilde{\rho})$ (dashed line). The red, blue and green colors refer to the solutions in the exponential potential, $V_1$ and $V_2$ regimes, respectively. We set $\alpha = 0.5$, $m_1 = 0.98\Phi_\star$ and $m_2 = 7.11\Phi_\star$, so that $\Phi_2 = -2.34\Phi_\star$ and $\Phi_{\rm M} = -2.35\Phi_\star$. The two matching points (black dots) are located at $\tilde{\rho} = 1$ and $\tilde{\rho}_2 = 5$.} \label{fig:fluctuation_piecewise}
elements.sent-2411-133: The intersection point $\{0.98, 7.11\}$ represents the location where all of the conditions [$c_8 = 0$, $\bar{\Phi (\tilde{\rho ) = \Phi_2$ and Eq.~(\ref{eq:con_bg )] are simultaneously satisfied.}}}
